ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for roasted pork belly as a food item; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 small portion (approximately 150g), suitable for 1–2 persons as a side dish
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical small serving of roasted pork in Singapore is approximately 150g, often ordered as a side dish alongside rice or noodles for one to two persons.

nutrient_source.title: Roasted Pork (Siu Yuk) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Roasted Pork (烧肉). All per-100g values are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for roasted pork belly (NDB 10006) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; these are documented as adapted values.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — food contains negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Roasted pork (siu yuk) contains 0.0g carbohydrate per 100g per HPB data. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. The numeric value 0 is used as a sentinel to satisfy the requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric; gi_category is set to 'not_applicable' to signal that GI testing is not meaningful for this food. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by calculation (GL = GI × carbohydrate_g / 100; carbohydrate_g = 0).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050mg per 100g — well above the 600mg/100g threshold for hypertension concern
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit or avoid roasted pork due to its very high sodium content of 1050mg/100g; even a 150g serving delivers approximately 1575mg sodium, representing over 68% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate eliminates glycaemic spike risk, but saturated fat content may impair long-term insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Roasted pork poses no acute glycaemic risk due to zero carbohydrate content, making it compatible with blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; however, regular consumption should be moderated given its saturated fat content (~3.8g/100g), which may impair insulin sensitivity over time, and its very high sodium load warrants caution in diabetic patients with concurrent hypertension or nephropathy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050mg/100g), saturated fat (~3.8g/100g), cholesterol (89mg/100g), and trans fat (~0.1g/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Roasted pork is not recommended for individuals with cardiovascular disease due to its combination of very high sodium (1050mg/100g), significant saturated fat, dietary cholesterol (89mg/100g), and detectable trans fat, all of which contribute to elevated blood pressure, LDL cholesterol, and arterial inflammation; consumption should be occasional and portion-controlled.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid from red meat drives pro-inflammatory TNF-alpha and IL-6 pathways; very high sodium exacerbates joint swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise roasted pork intake as its red meat arachidonic acid content promotes systemic inflammation via TNF-alpha and IL-6 pathways, and its very high sodium content (1050mg/100g) further worsens RA-related tissue oedema and joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted Pork, known in Cantonese as Siu Yuk (烧肉 / 燒肉) and in the Hokkien dialect as Sio Bak, is a classic Chinese barbecue dish widely popular in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora in Southeast Asia. It is prepared by marinating pork belly — a cut prized for its layered fat and lean meat — with salt, five-spice powder, white pepper, and sometimes rice wine or vinegar, then roasting it at high heat to achieve a deeply savoury interior and a distinctively crispy, blistered crackling skin. The result is a dish with rich umami flavour, a satisfying contrast between the crunchy skin and tender, juicy meat layers, and a pronounced saltiness from the marinade. In Singapore, siu yuk is commonly served as a hawker centre dish alongside steamed rice, char siew (barbecue pork), and roasted duck, or as part of a mixed roast platter. Nutritionally, roasted pork is high in protein (19.1g/100g) and moderate in fat (10.5g/100g), with zero carbohydrate and zero sugar, making it glycaemically inert. However, its sodium content is very high at 1050mg/100g, which is a significant concern for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or chronic kidney disease. The saturated fat (~3.8g/100g) and cholesterol (89mg/100g) content are relevant considerations for cardiovascular health. Glycemic index is not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content. This dish is not halal and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.
common_user_queries: How many calories in roasted pork?
Is roasted pork suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat siu yuk if I have high blood pressure?
What is the sodium content of roasted pork?
Is sio bak the same as siu yuk?
What is 烧肉 in English?
Is roasted pork high in fat?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for roasted pork belly, which is an approved secondary source. All adaptations are documented.
notes: carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — roasted pork belly contains no carbohydrate by composition (naturally zero). sugar_g is naturally zero as this is a plain roasted meat product with no added sugars in the standard preparation. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is a pure meat product. Saturated fat (3.8g/100g), monounsaturated fat (4.6g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (1.2g/100g), and trans fat (0.1g/100g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for roasted pork belly (NDB 10006) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; these are documented as adapted values. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed vitamin, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific HPB roasted pork entry are not reported in the HPB Foodo database, and no exact-match entry in USDA FoodData Central provides all these values for the Singapore preparation style. Glycemic index is set to numeric 0 as a sentinel value for a food with negligible carbohydrates (0.0g/100g per HPB); gi_category is set to 'not_applicable' to correctly signal that GI testing is not meaningful for this food. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by calculation. The input record listed the condition key as 'diabetes'; this has been corrected to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per Veritas naming rules. The rheumatoid arthritis health context from the input has been preserved as an additional condition. Trans fat caution in the original input (0.1g/100g) is reflected in the adapted trans_fat_g value from USDA SR Legacy.
